input_excerpt: We had an aircraft return with an intermittent fault. My team worked through it and got the jet back up before the next flight window.

findings.title: Your individual contribution is invisible
findings.why: For a Technical Operations Manager role, the reader is testing your judgment and technical decisions, not the group's. "My team worked through it" collapses everything into a collective effort, so the interviewer cannot tell what you personally diagnosed, decided, or directed. A manager candidate who disappears behind "my team" reads as unable to show ownership.
findings.task: Name the specific decisions and actions that were yours: what you inspected, what you ruled out, and what call you made. Keep the outcome the same; just separate your part from the team's.

findings.title: The technical problem-solving is missing
findings.why: The question asks how you solved a difficult technical problem, and this is the core of what the employer is testing: competence. "An intermittent fault" states there was a problem but shows none of the diagnostic reasoning that makes it impressive. A civilian technical reader cannot see how hard the problem was or how you narrowed it down.
findings.task: Add the concrete steps you took to isolate the fault — what symptoms you saw, what you tested, and how you confirmed the cause. Do not invent detail; recall the actual sequence.

findings.title: The pressure and stakes are unstated
findings.why: The question specifically asks about working "under pressure," so the interviewer wants to see the stakes and time constraint. "Before the next flight window" hints at a deadline but never says how much time you had or what would have failed if you missed it. Without stakes, the reader cannot gauge whether this was genuinely high-pressure.
findings.task: State the actual time constraint and the consequence of missing it (for example, what mission, sortie, or commitment was at risk). Use only facts you can defend.

findings.title: Aviation shorthand may not translate
findings.why: A civilian hiring reader may not know what "back up" involved — a repair, a sign-off, a return to flight-ready status. Borrowed operational shorthand leaves the result vague even though the underlying fact is real.
findings.task: Describe in plain terms what "back up" meant technically (repaired, tested, cleared for flight) so a non-aviation reader understands the outcome.

findings.title: No learning or calibration shown
findings.quote: My team worked through it and got the jet back up
findings.why: For a management-track role, interviewers value candidates who reflect on what a difficult problem taught them. The answer ends at the fix with no takeaway, so the reader sees a result but no growth or process improvement.
findings.task: Add one sentence on what you took from this — a process change, a diagnostic lesson, or how it shaped how you lead troubleshooting now.

note: The response is on-topic but too compressed to show your individual judgment, the technical difficulty, or the stakes an interviewer needs to see for a management role.
